name: Cova de sa Terra
starting_point: Upper part of the Costa d'en Blanes urbanization
description: Located near Cova dets Albons, this N/S oriented cave follows the typical Sierra morphology of fracture ramps with subsequent widening. The Sala de ses Espeleotemes features wall and floor flowstones with various stalagmites, while Sa Rampa descends 14 m at a 45% gradient. Ceramic remains suggest it was historically used as a water source. The cave hosts bats and is considered a site of special speleological interest.
full_description:
At the base of a small escarpment near the Cova dets Albons, the mouth of this cave opens, hidden behind vegetation that conceals a small sinkhole. The orientation of the cavity is N/S, following the same direction as its neighbor Cova de sa Sorpresa. Its morphology does not differ from many others in the sierra, one of its main characteristics being fractures in ramp form with a subsequent widening, only to then end in a closure with no possibility of continuation.

Sala de sa Entrada (Entrance Room)

Just after crossing the entrance, we access a small room on a ramp of 8 x 9 x 2.5 m whose deepest area is occupied by a large number of stones that prevent its continuation. The room lacks interest, although in another time it surely had some as a place where people who made their living in the area would come for water, as attested by some ceramic remains found in the room.

Sala de ses Espeleotemes (Speleothem Room)

To the east, a narrow yet short gallery allows us to access the Speleothem Room. To do so, we must pay the utmost attention, since at the exit of the aforementioned gallery, one must make a small jump until placing our feet on a small stalagmite that positions us in the room — a loss of balance during the jump would surely cause us to fall down a steep slope until reaching the deepest area of the cave. The dimensions of the room are 7 x 12 x 2.5 m. It is a practically horizontal room with a certain amount of concretion, where the parietal flowstones in the NW area stand out, as well as the floor flowstones that have cemented the stones on the room's floor and upon which stalagmites of different sizes and thicknesses have developed. Some small rimstone pools can also be found, though they are empty since the cave's activity is nonexistent. Ceiling speleothems are also quite abundant, though not of great beauty.

Sa Rampa (The Ramp)

With dimensions of 16 x 8 x 3 m and a ramp of approximately 45% gradient, it descends for 14 m over a cemented floor where deposited stones are found that can tumble at the slightest touch of the rope. Once at the bottom, the surface is covered by materials that have accumulated over time, blocking the way. However, along the western side, one can still continue descending with great difficulty about 3 more meters until reaching a small inaccessible shaft.

The Cova de sa Terra is the place chosen by some bat specimens to roost, so they should not be disturbed.

The Cova de sa Terra can be considered a natural site of special speleological interest.
